title: Basic routine observation
text: For mild or vague changes, first observe the animal as a whole: appetite, water intake, dung, standing or walking, cud chewing, milk change, and behavior. If the animal is eating, drinking, passing dung, and standing normally, the first step is careful monitoring and checking feed, water, weather, and stress changes.

title: When routine signs become concerning
text: Routine observation should escalate when the animal stops eating completely, cannot stand normally, has breathing trouble, severe weakness, high fever, repeated diarrhea, severe swelling, or distress. Do not diagnose from one vague sign alone; combine the sign with behavior, appetite, water, dung, and movement.

title: First-line support scope
text: First-line farmer support should prevent worsening while waiting or deciding on trained help. Useful actions are keeping the animal calm, reducing stress, checking the environment, observing key signs, and avoiding rough handling. First aid is not the same as treatment and should not turn into medicine, injection, surgery, or forced feeding.

title: Clean water access
text: Dairy animals need reliable access to clean drinking water. If water intake changes but the animal is otherwise normal, first check whether water is clean, fresh, reachable, and not too hot or dirty. Also check feed change, heat, shade, dung, appetite, and behavior before assuming disease.

title: Do not diagnose from water alone
text: Drinking more or less water alone does not confirm a disease. Ask one important check: is the animal eating normally, passing dung normally, chewing cud, standing normally, and showing no fever or breathing trouble? Escalate only if red flags such as severe weakness, high fever, breathing trouble, or severe diarrhea appear.

title: Heat and water routine check
text: Hot weather can change water needs, appetite, comfort, and milk yield. In heat, check shade, airflow, water availability, feed timing, and whether the animal is breathing normally. A mild milk drop with normal eating and behavior can be monitored, but fast breathing, collapse, severe weakness, or not drinking needs urgent trained help.

title: Moldy or damp feed warning
text: Moldy, damp, clumped, or bad-smelling feed should be treated as suspicious and should not be casually fed. If feed became wet after rain or storage was damp, remove the suspicious feed from use until checked. Give clean dry feed and clean water, and observe whether more animals show reduced appetite, weakness, milk drop, or loose dung.

title: Feed storage practical checks
text: Feed safety starts with dry storage. Check whether the feed is dry, not clumped, not moldy, and not smelling unusual. Feed changes should be gradual when possible. If several animals become dull or reduce feed after the same feed batch, treat it as a group warning and seek trained advice.

title: Do not confirm toxin exposure
text: Do not confirm aflatoxin, poisoning, or toxin exposure from symptoms alone. The safe farmer-facing response is to stop using suspicious spoiled feed, keep water and clean feed available, note how many animals are affected, and contact trained animal-health support if animals are weak, off-feed, or multiple animals are affected.

title: Clean milk routine
text: Clean milk production depends on clean animals, clean hands, clean utensils, and hygienic milking. Before milking, keep the udder area and vessels clean and avoid dirty water, dirty cloth, or contaminated containers. This supports hygiene but does not certify milk as safe when milk looks abnormal.

title: Abnormal milk boundary
text: If milk has visible clots, blood-like appearance, watery change, or bad smell, keep that milk separate for now. Check udder heat, swelling, pain, fever, appetite, and whether milk reduction is sudden or repeated. Do not guarantee that abnormal milk is safe to sell, drink, or feed.

title: Udder red flags concept
text: Udder inflammation problems can show as abnormal milk and udder changes such as heat, swelling, pain, firmness, or reduced milk. The assistant should not diagnose mastitis from text alone. It should ask about udder heat, swelling, pain, fever, appetite, and milk appearance, then advise trained help when red flags are present.

title: Milk sale pressure
text: When milk looks abnormal or the animal has mouth lesions, fever, udder swelling, or other disease signs, do not make a safety guarantee. Keep milk separate and ask local dairy/veterinary guidance. The assistant should not say milk is safe for home use or sale based only on a text description.

title: Bloat danger signs
text: A tight or swollen left belly with restlessness, repeated sitting/standing, fast breathing, or severe discomfort after feed can be urgent in cattle or buffalo. The safe response is to keep the animal calm, avoid forcing anything by mouth, and call trained animal-health help quickly.

title: Bloat unsafe myths
text: For suspected bloat, do not advise oil, drenching, pipe insertion, puncturing, cutting, or force-feeding. These actions can harm the animal if done by an untrained person. If the animal is severely bloated, restless, or breathing fast, urgent trained help is needed.

title: Bloat check-first wording
text: If belly swelling is only suspected, first check whether the left side is clearly tight or swollen, whether the animal is restless, breathing fast, not eating, or repeatedly trying to lie down. If these signs are absent and the animal is normal, use cautious observation rather than declaring bloat.

title: Minor wound first check
text: For a minor surface wound, first check whether it is only on the surface or whether it is deep, bleeding heavily, swelling, hot, very painful, or foul-smelling. Keep handling gentle and clean. If the wound is deep, spreading, has pus or maggots, or the animal is unwell, seek trained help.

title: Clean water only wound support
text: For a small dirty surface wound, clean water can be used gently to remove visible dirt. Do not scrub hard or push material deeper into the wound. Keep the area clean and observe for swelling, heat, bad smell, pus, worsening pain, or the animal becoming dull or off-feed.

title: Wound irritant myths
text: Do not apply irritants or dirty home materials such as chili, ash, soil, dung, unknown powder, strong chemicals, or harsh products to a wound. These can worsen irritation or contamination. Use clean handling and seek trained help if swelling, pus, maggots, heat, or bad smell appears.

title: Maggots or flies on wound
text: Flies or maggots on a wound are a serious warning. Do not apply chili, ash, kerosene, acid, or unknown chemicals. Keep the animal in a cleaner place, avoid rough handling, and contact trained animal-health help. This needs more than casual home observation.

title: Skin marks routine approach
text: For skin marks, rough hair, or mild itching while the animal is eating and behaving normally, first observe whether marks are spreading, swollen, wounded, bleeding, or attracting flies. Check for rope rubbing, insects, wet bedding, recent chemical spray, or feed/environment change. Do not apply random medicine or harsh home remedies.

title: Skin red flags
text: Skin signs need trained help if redness spreads quickly, swelling becomes painful, wounds open, pus or bad smell appears, flies gather, the animal develops fever, or appetite drops. Avoid diagnosing the exact skin disease from text alone; focus on observation and safe escalation.

title: Calf routine feeding transition
text: For a healthy calf that is slow to start dry feed but alert and active, check whether the feed is fresh, easy to reach, and palatable. Also check clean water, comfort, and stress. Do not force the calf to eat; observe whether it remains bright and continues normal activity.

title: Calf loose dung monitoring
text: If a calf has loose dung but is still standing, alert, and drinking, monitor closely. Check frequency of loose dung, energy, drinking, eyes, mouth moisture, and whether it is becoming weak. Keep the calf clean, dry, calm, and with access to clean water. Escalate if loose dung continues, weakness appears, or drinking stops.

title: Weak calf red flags
text: A calf that is very dull, not drinking milk, weak while standing, unable to stand, or showing ongoing diarrhea needs trained help quickly. Do not force-feed liquids or give medicines/doses without trained guidance. Keep the calf warm or comfortable according to weather and reduce stress while arranging help.

title: Calving delay danger
text: If the water bag has been visible for a long time, the animal is straining, or the calf is not coming out, treat it as urgent. Keep the animal calm and clean, avoid crowding, and call trained veterinary help. Do not wait casually if the animal is distressed or progress has stopped.

title: Do not pull hard
text: Do not pull hard during calving, especially if calf position seems abnormal or progress has stopped. Forceful pulling can injure the mother and calf. The safe farmer-facing action is to keep the area clean and calm and get trained help urgently.

title: Post-calving observation
text: After calving, watch appetite, water intake, behavior, milk, standing, discharge, and whether the animal seems restless or weak. Not eating after calving, repeated restlessness, fever, bad-smelling discharge, or inability to stand should be treated as a red flag needing trained help.

title: Dog bite or saliva exposure
text: Dog bite or saliva exposure involving broken skin, open wounds, eyes, or mouth is a human-safety concern. Keep children away from saliva or bite areas, avoid casual handling, and seek local medical guidance for exposed people. Do not rule out rabies from appearance alone.

title: Rabies boundary
text: The assistant should not give rabies vaccine schedules, doses, or certainty that exposure is safe. Trained medical staff decide exposure management. For animal bite/saliva situations, answer with immediate safety and medical escalation, not casual reassurance.

title: Multiple animals with mouth or foot signs
text: Multiple animals with mouth sores, heavy salivation, lameness, fever, or sudden milk drop should be treated as a herd-health warning. Do not take affected animals to market or mix them casually. Contact official animal-health support or a local veterinarian for guidance.

title: Outbreak uncertainty and market boundary
text: When several animals show similar concerning signs, avoid guessing the disease or giving market safety clearance. Keep the response focused on separation, observation, and official animal-health contact. The assistant should not certify movement, sale, milk, or meat safety.

title: Medicine pressure refusal
text: If the user asks for injection, antibiotic, syrup, tablet, or dose, do not provide medicine names or amounts. Give a brief refusal, ask for key observable signs if needed, and suggest trained veterinary guidance. Offer only safe support such as clean water access, calm environment, and monitoring red flags.

title: Medicine shop boundary
text: Medicine-shop pressure should not override veterinary assessment. The assistant should not confirm antibiotics, injections, human medicines, or doses. It can ask what signs are present, whether the animal is eating, drinking, standing, breathing normally, and whether there is fever, diarrhea, swelling, wound, or severe weakness.

title: Slow walking after grazing
text: If the animal walks slowly after grazing but eats, drinks, and stands normally, first check for visible injury, swelling, heat, thorn, stone, or tenderness in the feet and legs. Give rest and observe. Escalate if it cannot bear weight, swelling increases, there is a wound, fever, or the limp worsens.

title: Mild dung change
text: A mild dung change for one day should be checked with feed, water, weather, appetite, and behavior. Ask whether the animal is eating, drinking, chewing cud, standing normally, and whether feed changed. Escalate if diarrhea is repeated, watery, bloody, foul-smelling, or with weakness, dehydration, fever, or not eating.

title: Dry nose or mild nasal water
text: A dry nose or slight clear nasal discharge alone does not confirm serious disease. Check appetite, water, fever, cough, breathing, eye/nose discharge color, and whether the animal is active. Escalate if discharge becomes thick or foul, breathing changes, fever appears, or appetite drops.

title: Cough and nasal discharge check
text: For cough or nasal discharge, check whether the animal has fever, fast or difficult breathing, thick discharge, low appetite, or weakness. Keep the animal in a clean, airy, low-stress place. Do not give cough syrup, human tablets, antibiotics, or dose advice without trained veterinary guidance.

title: Restless sounds at night
text: Restlessness or unusual sounds can come from discomfort, environment, feed, heat, injury, or early illness. Check feed/water, dung, belly swelling, breathing, walking, wounds, and whether the animal is lying and rising normally. Escalate if restlessness is severe, belly is tight, breathing is fast, or the animal cannot stand.

title: Ear or tail shaking
text: Ear or tail shaking can be a response to flies, irritation, heat, insects, or discomfort. Check for wounds, swelling, discharge, ticks, flies, skin marks, and whether appetite and behavior are normal. Escalate if there is severe pain, wound, pus, fever, or behavior change.

title: Licking wall or soil
text: Repeated licking of wall or soil should be observed with feed quality, mineral access, appetite, dung, and behavior. Do not diagnose a deficiency from this sign alone. Check whether feed changed, salt/mineral access is appropriate, and whether the animal also has weakness, poor appetite, weight loss, or abnormal dung.

title: Animal down and breathing fast
text: An animal that suddenly falls, is not getting up, or is breathing fast is urgent. Do not force water, feed, oil, or medicine into the mouth. Keep the animal calm and in a safe, ventilated place, check for obvious danger around it, and call trained veterinary help immediately.

title: Drooling and not eating
text: Heavy salivation with mouth sores, not eating, fever, or lameness can be a herd-health warning. Do not diagnose the exact disease from text alone, but avoid casual mixing or market movement. Contact local animal-health support, especially if more than one animal is affected.

title: Photo or text cannot confirm disease
text: A photo or short text description can help identify visible red flags, but it cannot safely confirm disease, medicine, milk safety, or exact treatment. The assistant should describe what to check, ask one important follow-up if needed, and escalate when red flags are visible or described.

title: Short rural helper style
text: For farmer-facing answers, keep language short, practical, and close to the user's language. Prefer one safe first step, one or two checks, and one escalation condition. Avoid long legal disclaimers, unrelated warnings, and generic policy spam.
